questions.prompt: Why do most algo bots use IB Gateway instead of TWS for IBKR connectivity?

choices.text: IB Gateway is a lightweight, GUI-less version of TWS that speaks the same API protocol. For unattended bots running on VPSs or headless servers, Gateway uses far less memory, has nothing to crash visually, requires no display server, and is more reliable for long-running automated processes. TWS is the full desktop trading workstation — overkill (and resource-heavy) when all you need is the API bridge. Standard pattern: Gateway runs on the bot's server; bot connects via localhost:7497 (paper) or 7496 (live). Both auto-login solutions (IBController, ib-gateway-docker) target Gateway, not TWS.

questions.explanation: Gateway is lightweight, GUI-less — purpose-built for unattended bots on servers.

questions.prompt: Bot loses TCP connection to IB Gateway mid-trading-day. What's the correct recovery?

choices.text: Reconnect with backoff and reconcile state — DON'T blindly resubmit orders. The orders you sent before the disconnect may still be live on IBKR's side; resubmitting duplicates them, doubling your position. Correct flow: (1) attempt reconnect with exponential backoff, (2) on reconnect, call `reqOpenOrders` and `reqPositions` to fetch IBKR's view of state, (3) reconcile against your local intent — keep orders that match, cancel duplicates, refresh tracking. Connection loss is recoverable; the danger is the bot acting on stale assumptions. Same principle applies to any broker API: never assume your last-known state survived a disconnect.

questions.explanation: Reconnect with backoff, fetch broker's view of open orders/positions, reconcile. Never blindly resubmit.
